questions.question_text: A buyer's lender requires the escrow holder to be licensed. Which agency licenses independent escrow companies in California?
questions.correct_answer: California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation
distractors: California Department of Real Estate
California Department of Insurance
California Bureau of Real Estate Appraisers
questions.explanation: Independent escrow companies in California are licensed by the Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI). The DRE licenses real estate brokers, who may conduct non-independent escrow.

questions.question_text: During an escrow, the seller's lender payoff arrives but the buyer's loan funds have not yet been received. The escrow officer wants to close anyway. Which rule applies?
questions.correct_answer: No disbursement is permitted until all conditions are met
distractors: Escrow may close once the seller's side is complete
The escrow officer can use seller proceeds to bridge the gap
A 24-hour grace period is allowed by DFPI regulations
questions.explanation: Escrow instructions are binding, and the neutral third party may not disburse funds until all conditions are satisfied. Closing before buyer funds arrive would breach the escrow officer's fiduciary duty.

questions.question_text: A real estate attorney in California wants to handle escrow for her client's transaction without an independent escrow license. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, attorneys are exempt from independent escrow licensing
distractors: No, all escrow must be handled by DFPI-licensed companies
Yes, but only for commercial transactions over $1 million
No, only licensed brokers may conduct non-independent escrow
questions.explanation: California law exempts attorneys, licensed brokers, and title companies from the independent escrow licensing requirement when acting in their professional capacity. These are classified as non-independent escrow holders.

questions.question_text: After escrow closes, the buyer discovers the seller signed conflicting escrow instructions that were never disclosed. Which characteristic of escrow instructions is most relevant?
questions.correct_answer: Instructions are binding on all parties once signed
distractors: Instructions are suggestions until title transfers
Only the escrow officer's version of instructions controls
Conflicting instructions automatically void the transaction
questions.explanation: Escrow instructions are legally binding contracts once signed. Conflicting or fraudulent instructions are a breach of those obligations. The escrow officer serves as a neutral fiduciary to both parties.

questions.question_text: An escrow officer receives the buyer's loan funds and all signed documents. She confirms no contingencies remain open. What is her next proper step?
questions.correct_answer: Disburse funds and authorize recording of documents
distractors: Wait 3 business days before disbursing funds
Request seller confirmation before closing
Notify the DRE that escrow is ready to close
questions.explanation: Once all conditions in the escrow instructions are satisfied, the escrow officer may disburse funds and authorize recording. There is no mandatory waiting period once all conditions are met.

questions.question_text: A lender in Southern California orders a title report for a refinance. The lender will likely require which type of title insurance policy?
distractors: CLTA standard coverage policy
Joint protection policy
Preliminary title report only
questions.explanation: Lenders require ALTA extended coverage, which protects against unrecorded claims, survey issues, and rights of parties in possession — risks not covered by the CLTA standard policy issued to buyers.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchasing in San Francisco asks her agent who typically pays for the owner's title insurance policy. Which regional custom applies in Northern California?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer pays for her own CLTA policy
distractors: Seller pays for the buyer's CLTA policy
Lender pays because it benefits from title protection
Cost is always split 50/50 between buyer and seller
questions.explanation: In Northern California, custom is for the buyer to pay for the owner's title insurance policy (CLTA). In Southern California, the seller typically pays. These are customs, not laws, and can be negotiated.

questions.question_text: A title search reveals a gap in the chain of title from 1952. A CLTA standard policy is issued. Which risk is NOT covered by this policy?
questions.correct_answer: Rights of a tenant in possession not shown in records
distractors: Recorded liens from prior owners
Encumbrances appearing in public records
Forged deed recorded in the county records
questions.explanation: CLTA standard coverage protects against defects in recorded public records. It does not cover unrecorded claims such as rights of parties in possession — that protection is provided only by ALTA extended coverage.

questions.question_text: A title company issues a preliminary title report two weeks before closing. What is the primary purpose of this report?
questions.correct_answer: It discloses the condition of title before insurance is issued
distractors: It insures title from the date of issuance
It replaces the need for a full title insurance policy
It establishes the legal description for the grant deed
questions.explanation: A preliminary title report (prelim) discloses the current state of title — including liens, easements, and exceptions — before the title insurance policy is issued. It is not itself an insurance policy.

questions.question_text: ALTA extended title insurance differs from CLTA standard coverage primarily because ALTA also covers which risk?
distractors: Forged documents in public records
Recorded judgment liens against the seller
Unpaid property taxes shown in county records
questions.explanation: ALTA extended coverage adds protection for unrecorded claims, survey issues, and rights of parties in possession that cannot be found in public records. Both policies cover recorded defects, liens, and encumbrances.

questions.question_text: A California seller uses a grant deed to convey property. What implied warranties does the grant deed automatically provide?
questions.correct_answer: The grantor has not previously conveyed the property and discloses no undisclosed encumbrances
distractors: The grantor owns the property free of all liens
The grantor warrants title against all future claims
The property has no physical defects and meets all building codes
questions.explanation: California's grant deed carries two implied warranties by statute: (1) the grantor has not previously conveyed the same property to another, and (2) the property is free from encumbrances made by the grantor that are not disclosed.

questions.question_text: A divorcing spouse wants to release any interest she may have in a property without making any guarantees about title. Which deed is most appropriate?
distractors: Grant deed
Warranty deed
Sheriff's deed
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has, if any, with no warranties whatsoever. It is commonly used to clear title disputes, dissolve co-ownership, or release marital interests.

questions.question_text: A property in Los Angeles sells for $650,000. What is the documentary transfer tax owed to the county at $1.10 per $1,000 of value?
questions.explanation: $650,000 ÷ $1,000 = 650 units × $1.10 = $715.00. California charges $1.10 per $1,000 of consideration (equivalent to $0.55 per $500). Cities may levy an additional city transfer tax on top of this amount.

questions.question_text: A parent transfers a home into a revocable living trust for estate planning purposes. Is documentary transfer tax owed on this transfer?
questions.correct_answer: No, transfers into revocable trusts are exempt
distractors: Yes, all deed recordings trigger transfer tax
Yes, but at a reduced rate of $0.55 per $1,000
Only if the property's assessed value exceeds $1 million
questions.explanation: California Revenue and Taxation Code exempts certain transfers from documentary transfer tax, including transfers into a revocable living trust where the transferor retains beneficial interest. Spouse transfers and foreclosure deeds also qualify for exemptions.

questions.question_text: Two deeds are recorded on the same day for the same property. A deed recorded at 9:00 AM and a second deed recorded at 2:00 PM — which controls priority?
questions.correct_answer: The deed recorded first in time controls priority
distractors: The deed with the earlier execution date controls
Both deeds are equally valid and split interest
The county recorder must void the later deed
questions.explanation: California's recording system establishes priority by the order of recording. The deed recorded first — 9:00 AM — establishes priority over the later recording, regardless of when each deed was signed or executed.
